sentences.text: Well, the theater-- the Hebrew theater put on a performance called [NON-ENGLISH], by Pinski.
We did not have the book, but the teacher Dimentman reconstructed it from memory.
And I played a small part in it.
The-- the Hebrew choir was headed by a conductor called Dumarshkin.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had sopranos, alts, tenors, and was baritones.
We sang like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 'Herbstlied' by Mendelssohn and song of autumn-- 'Autumn Song' by Mendelssohn.
We sang the 'Grand March' of And we song-- we sang-- we were singing also many Hebrew Zionist songs.
Once or twice a week.

It did, because I assume their line was that as long as the Judenrat exists and as long as people work and don't run away and don't go into the forests, then they should have some release, some entertainment.
And this was the entertainment.
And I think that the-- the Germans knew about it as well.
This was contrary to let's say the Kovno ghetto, where the leadership helped the underground.
In Vilna ghetto, I don't think it was like that.
Yes.
There were from time to time Jews killed, one or two, for various so-called offenses.
But by and large it was the quiet period.
And anyway, I was concentrating on trying to get out.
And I had a weapon.
I was about to go out with Dr.
Leon Bernstein and his group.
But in the meantime, his group fell apart.
I will not go into details.
And he himself joined the FBO.
In the meantime, I had to leave the ghetto quite abruptly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because the man who sold me the weapon was caught.
And I was tipped off by Chaim Trapido, the director of the [NON-ENGLISH] in Vilna.
It was a Kovno newspaper.

And I was tipped off and they told me I have no time to go to my room to take the weapon, so a friend of mine and I went to a place where Professor Stakauskas sent us, because he apparently had some information that something is going to happen in the ghetto.
I knew, yes.
But I have never been there at that time.
Well, we knew because the-- the FBO and others said that people are not taking to any other working places.
They are taken to Ponary and being shot.
And we knew also about the Ninth Fort in Kovno, because there was contact between the ghettos.
I knew that Jews were killed.
They were shot.
Both in Kovno and in Vilna, in Vilna and Ponary and Kovno in the Ninth Fort.
Well, I may have met him once, but I had no direct contact.
I met his-- I knew his daughter.
Ada.
She was half-Jewish, because her mother was a Lithuanian.
He was the head of the ghetto, was ghetto chef.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my opinion, which I kept to myself, was that he was a very tragic figure, that he's trying to save some Jews at the expense of others.
I wasn't quite sure whether morally or according to halakah, to Jewish law, one should assume that position.
But of course, it was easy for me to say.
And this may also be practically hindsight, like a Monday morning quarterback.
It's difficult to judge Gens.
Gens' intentions were good.
Dessler I believe, his assistant, the police chief for Vilna, I believe was already unwillingly-- he became a Gestapo agent.
Dessler?
D-E-S-S-L-E-R.
Unwillingly, he became a collaborator of-- Gens actually was shot by the Germans.
I don't know.
But I know he was shot.
I don't remember.
I don't think I was in the ghetto at that time.
I-- I-- we walked out of the ghetto.
We took off our jackets.
It was a hot summer day.
It was also the 13th of July, because everything happened on the same day.
And we walked out and we went south.
And after about 10 days of many eventful things that happened, we were shot at and somebody tried to snatch my shoes, my boots.
And my boots had all-- not only the boots, but also my money.
So--
I was injured, yes, in my foot.
And then afterwards in Romania I had to-- I jumped a train when Russians tried to check my documents and they were not very happy with them.
I jumped and I injured my nose.
Yes.
Well, they-- they were around these days, I don't remember exactly, that they were-- there was a question of Wittenberg.

Wittenberg was the-- the commander of the FPO, who was-- whose name was given away by the Lithuanian Communists in town that were captured by the Nazis, something that I wasn't surprised at all, knowing the nature of the Lithuanian approach to Communists and to Russians the last 500 years.
I don't want to dwell too much on that.
Well, because that's what the Germans said.
They either give us Wittenberg or we'll destroy the ghetto.
And that time the--
-- FPO declared mobilization of its members.
They wants freed Wittenberg from the hands of the Jewish police that wanted to give him to Germans.
The Germans came back and threatened to destroy the ghetto.
At that time Gens and Dessler and the Jewish ghetto leadership [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] called for a meeting of many people and said, look, you all want to be destroyed because of one man?
Help us to find leader-- Wittenberg.
Now.
I don't remember.
I-- I even don't know whether this is information that I knew at the time.
I knew some.
I saw some people with-- with weapons and I saw-- but, oh, it was later that I read about it.
And you must understand that two of my close friends are both Holocaust researchers.
Must have been in July or something like that.
I guess so.
This you can check the details.
I know-- I knew Josef Glazman.
He was the [NON-ENGLISH] of Betar in Lithuania.
And he was in Vilna in the police.
But of course, he was working with the underground, with the FPO.
He-- he was killed when they went to-- to the woods.
But on the way, I believe there were-- they were surprised by a German unit.
That's right.
Yes.
I don't want to comment on-- on him.
I knew him very well.
I knew him from home.
I knew his sister, Miriam.
I knew them from [NON-ENGLISH].
To go to the woods to survive, to outlive Hitler, and to go to Palestine.
Of course it was difficult.
It was dangerous.
I nearly lost my life, because if I didn't get out-- after I left, the ghetto administration published that I was caught and-- and killed by the Germans.
So when I came back in 1944, some people thought that I came back from the dead.
They said, what are you doing here?
That's what they said.
I don't know what they published about me, but I'm here.
From Professor, yes, Stakauskas.
He was a priest and a professor of bibliography.
It says [NON-ENGLISH].
It is advisable quickly to change your living quarters.
[NON-ENGLISH].
If you will have some difficulties-- [NON-ENGLISH] and this address.
If there will be some difficulties, please contact the priest Gediminas Peletskis And they gave the address.
I forgot the address.
I tried when I was after the war in '91 or '92 in Vilna to get his address and his details from the curia.
But they did not have the documents, because the Russians have confiscated in the '80s all the archives of the-- of the bishop in Vilna, probably in all of Lithuania.
We were separated-- the shooting, my friend and I, and we-- we-- we got there separately.
And I didn't know all the time about my friend that he was alive, because the priest, although he was not trained in intelligence, knew very well how to separate things, was very smart.
And I didn't know until just before the German rule fell apart and we were about to be liberated and we could hear the guns of the front coming close.
We-- I didn't even know that he existed.
And he didn't know about me.
Professor 'Zirnofsky,' Zirnauskas, professor of chemistry, very talented.
He did not want to go to Palestine or to Israel.
He died a few years ago.
He was a professor of chemistry at the Vilnius University.
He was the dean of, actually, chemistry.
Zirnauskas, Z-I-R-N-A-U-S-K-A-S.
I don't remember his first name.
He sent me to-- to one farmer.

And afterwards, either the farmer was not happy to keep me, maybe he did not believe in my flimsy excuse, you know, that I was a Lithuanian from the area of Telz, I said, not from my hometown, and I was running away from the Germans.
Then alternatively I said I was looking for my parents that were expelled by the Russians.
And I was looking-- I heard that they jumped the train in this area.
So after that, he put me to another farmer.
The other farmer was called Jakimavicius.
And would you spell the name of that farmer?
J-A-K-I-M-A-V-I-C-I-U-S.
And there, after a while, the priest, when he visited me and discovered that I knew plenty about the Catholic religion, because sometimes I used to remain in class when they had religion classes.
I didn't have to go to the Jewish religion classes, because I was far ahead of-- of my teacher.
That was the rabbi's daughter.
And I was more ahead because I-- for eight years, I had a permanent rabbi training me every day.
So I used to stay.
And I knew about the catechism.
I knew other things, so I used to teach catechism to daughters of Jakimavicius.
And he was a Lithuanian and I taught them in Lithuanian.
What-- what-- But
I tried not to appear publicly and tried to keep to myself.
And I understood that in case of a really big inquiry would be a problem.
And there was, of course, some doubt about my being a Lithuanian Catholic.
And this is when the story with the pig came and actually helped me to establish myself.
I was invited to sviezines.
That means fresh, fresh blood.
And you do it by stabbing a pig and drinking its hot, gushing blood.
Sviezines.
S-V-I-E-Z-I-N-E-S.
And I had a hunch why I was invited, because normally, you give such an honor to an uncle or a father or-- or an important person or the priest.
I was given it because some people said, lets do it, because the priest says that he's related to the bishop and he's a refugee and we should help him, but he kind of has sad eyes.
He looks like a Jew.
But he doesn't speak like a Jew, said.
They said, OK, so we don't know.
So let's give him this test.
So they gave me the test.
And the same morning I prayed in Hebrew secretly.
And then I went to the party.
And of course, I-- I took a big knife and I hit the pig right into the throat, where I was supposed to, because I've seen the ceremony in Lithuania.
I didn't have to drink the blood before the war, because everybody knew I was Jewish.
This time, I drank the blood would great gusto.
And I don't know how I swallowed it.
And everybody else joined in.
And on the way back, when everybody was drunk from locally made liquor, my host said that-- don't feel so, so big.
It's not because you are a teacher or because you are so smart.
Simply they wanted to use this as a test to see if you are Jewish or not.
And thank god you're not.
I had false papers from the priest Gediminas Peletskis.
But of course, these were not-- these were papers of a village priest.
I believe Aleksas Tautkus.
A-L-E-K-S-A-S Tautkus, T-A-U-T-K-U-S.
Well, whenever Germans used to approach the-- the farm, I used to-- from the back, I always-- I had my place prepared.
And I used to hear a motor, because no-- none of the local people had a motor car.
So if it was a motor car it must have been either police or army.
So whenever I heard the-- and we were quite removed from the road.
I could easily sneak out into the woods, because it was a wooded area.
And I had a place prepared two, three miles away.
When everything quietened down, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they left, I used to come back.
And the farmer's wife once asked me, why did you go away?
I mean, are you OK?
I said, no.
You see, the Germans might draft me into the-- into work or into the Lithuanian battalions.
I don't want to go to the front.
I don't trust the Germans more than I trust the Russians.
I'm a Lithuanian nationalist.
And the Russians didn't-- didn't honor our freedom, neither did the Germans.
They did not keep their word that they're going to restore independent Lithuania.
Lithuania became another district of the eastern provinces.
We knew more or less the progress of the war, sometimes from the priest and sometimes from other people that were connected with the Armia Krajowa.
Armia Krajowa was a Polish underground.
There were two, Armia Krajowa and Armia Ludowa.
The Armia Krajowa was anti-Semitic and often killed Jews.
In yesterday's there was an article that you should-- I mean, one should read about what they did after the war.

Well, praying, singing quietly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Israeli songs, Hebrew songs that I knew, and thinking and planning my life, what I will do in Palestine after Hitler is defeated and I run away from the Russians.
Well, strange enough, I hoped to do exactly what I did, because I-- I wanted to work in the foreign ministry.
And that's where I ended up in Israel.
I was liberated on the 13th of July, 1944 by the Soviet army.
The whole front of the Russians-- of the Germans collapsed.
And all of a sudden-- all of a sudden, we found ourselves free.
At that time, since there were many Lithuanians and many units of partisans still on the loose, of Lithuanian partisans, the priest said, don't remain here.
It's dangerous.
Go back to a big town.
I said, I-- I was in Vilna.
He said, go back to Vilna.
A day before that introduced me again to my friend 'Zirnofsky,' and we went together back to Vilna.
Well, I found very few Jews.
And everybody was looking for somebody.
And I was looking for an apartment.
I don't think my apartment was in existence, so I was free.
So I found an empty house on Gedimino, on the nicest street.
And I made some changes, you know, a new lock and a few things.
I found some artisans and I got together a little furniture.
And I lived there and-- What did you do to support yourself in that time?
In the beginning, I still had a little money from my boots, the golden coins.
I had a few.
And then I started immediate to work.
And it was a Jewish community and I started to work for the Soviet government that re-established itself.
And I worked in the ministry of light industry. | used my work there to help Polish Jews-- I mean, Vilna Jews.
Vilna before 1939 belonged to Poland, so these people wanted to leave the Soviet Union, so they wanted to repatriate to Poland.
And for that they needed to document that they were not essential, their work was not essential for-- for the Ministry, so I gave them such documents.
The last document I gave was to Mrs.
Yoffe and to her little girl, who is now married in-- somewhere in New York.
Mrs.
Yoffe, Y-O-F-F-E, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her daughter Esti.
I believe-- I believe it was Esti, E-S-T-I.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She worked-- they both worked for me, for us, for the Ministry.
And I gave them the document.
And then I disappeared myself.
And in three days, we all were on a truck that we rented.

And they took us not on the highway, but on byways, to avoid-- to avoid the border, because although Poland-- the free Poland was, so to speak, a-- still under the Soviets, but it was different from the Soviet Union.
I guess in-- in July.
One year.
What-- when the Germans--
In May, in May of '45.
Yeah.
Well, everybody was happy that the Germans lost the war.
But I-- I frankly was more worried to get out.
What-- because Lithuanian Jews were in a predicament.
They couldn't get out like others, like Polish refugees Jews.
Or just from Vilna, because we were made Soviet citizens again.
No.
I decided the moment I came, in July '43-- '44, excuse me.
--when I--
Where did I go?
I went to Bialystock, B-I-A-L-Y-S-T-O-K.
And from Bialystok, we went to Lodz, L-O-D-Z.
And from there, we had already contact with the Berihah, with the-- with the Jewish underground that originated in Palestine.
They-- they helped us.
They simply started to take us in groups.
Our group was taken via Czechoslovakia, Hungary, to Romania, in the hope that-- to set sail from one of the Black Sea ports.
What happened was that the Russians prevented any ships from leaving.
And then they decided that we'll try Italy.
So they brought us from Romania via Austria from the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Russian part into the British part, under very clever tricks--
--that the Jewish Brigade group--
They put us in huge trucks of the Jewish Brigade group.
And when-- they had false papers and-- that send them there.
And nobody saw us inside the trucks.
And when the British military police stops stopped them and asked them, what-- what is their mission, they said, this is a very secret mission.
It's TTG.
So the British asked, what is TTG?
They say, [NON-ENGLISH], which means, [INAUDIBLE].
TGB is in Arabic the same thing, Geschaften means business.
And the-- the British didn't know.
And we-- we crossed through in Tarvisio.
We went to Mestre in Venice.
And then we felt really free for the first time.
And I remember we went to Mestre to the entertainment park.
And we rant those little motor cars, you know, electric cars that collide with one another, like children, you know.
And after that we were in Italy until the Berihah told us one day to go again using the British army Palestine units, Jewish units, trucks.
We went to Monopoli in southern Italy.
And we left for Palestine, which we reached before Rosh Hashanah, I believe.
